Just now

--

Episode 1: What Is AI, Anyway?
Every few weeks someone in my comments asks some version of the same question: “But what actually IS AI?” Not in the textbook sense — they’ve heard that a hundred times. They want to know what it really means, underneath all the noise, the hype, the hot takes, and the LinkedIn posts written like manifestos.
So I’m starting a series to answer exactly that. It’s called Artificially Yours — a name that felt right the moment I landed on it, because that’s really what this series is. AI, explained the way I’d explain it to a friend over coffee, not the way a research paper would explain it to a committee. No jargon dumps. No pretending I know things I don’t. Just the concepts, broken down, one at a time, until the whole picture starts making sense.
This is the first one. And before we go anywhere else, we have to start at the actual beginning.
So, what is AI?
Strip away the sci-fi movies and the killer robots and the “will it take my job” panic for a second. At its core, AI is just this: a way of teaching computers to notice patterns and make decisions based on them, without a human writing out every single rule by hand.
That’s it. That’s the whole idea.
Think about how you learned to recognize a dog. Nobody sat you down and gave you a checklist — four legs, tail, fur, this exact ear shape. You just saw enough dogs, in enough situations, and your brain built its own internal sense of “dog-ness.” AI does something similar, except instead of a brain, it’s using math — specifically statistics and something called neural networks — and instead of a childhood, it’s using massive piles of data.
You feed a system millions of examples. Pictures, sentences, numbers, transactions, whatever the task calls for. The system adjusts itself over and over, quietly getting better at spotting the pattern you want it to spot. Eventually, you show it something brand new, and it makes a prediction based on everything it’s absorbed. That prediction might be “this is spam,” or “this customer is about to cancel,” or “here’s the next word in this sentence.” All of it is the same basic trick, just aimed at different problems.
Why it feels new when it isn’t
Here’s the part most people don’t realize: none of this is actually new. The ideas behind AI go back to the 1950s. What’s new is that three things finally lined up at the same time — enormous amounts of data, enormous amounts of computing power, and clever new techniques for training these systems efficiently. That combination is why AI suddenly went from “interesting research topic” to “thing that’s writing your emails, editing your videos, and building your websites.”
It’s not magic. It’s not a mind. It’s pattern recognition running at a scale humans literally cannot do in our heads. That’s the whole shift.
The three flavors you’ll keep hearing about
You don’t need to memorize technical definitions, but these three terms will keep showing up, so it helps to have a mental shortcut for each:

• Machine Learning — the broad idea of a system learning from data instead of being explicitly programmed for every case.

• Deep Learning — a specific approach inside machine learning, using layered neural networks, which is what powers most of the AI you interact with today.

• Generative AI — systems (like the ones writing code with you, or generating images) that don’t just recognize patterns, they create new things based on them — text, images, music, code.
Every AI tool you’ve used is basically some combination of these three ideas, dressed up in a nice interface.
Why this series exists
I build with AI every single day — for content, for code, for client work, for Anirology itself. And the more I use it, the more I’ve realized that understanding what’s actually happening under the hood makes you dramatically better at using it. You stop treating it like a mysterious oracle and start treating it like a tool with logic you can actually predict and work with.
That’s the whole point of Artificially Yours. Not to make you a machine learning engineer overnight, but to make AI feel less like magic and more like something you understand — one concept, one post, at a time.
What’s coming next
This series is going to be turned into a proper newsletter soon, coming to you directly from Anirology — so you don’t have to go hunting for the next post, it’ll just land in your inbox. Future episodes will get into things like how large language models actually predict text, what a prompt is really doing behind the scenes, why AI sometimes confidently makes things up, and how tools like the ones I use daily are built on top of all this.
If episode one made even one confusing AI term click for you, that’s the goal working exactly as intended.
More soon. Artificially yours, Anirology
